There was this one conference I went to and it was all very structured on stage. And in that moment, this whole new level of respect and connection just opened up and it really changed how I saw them.

That moment by the coffee table sounds like it stripped away the polished facade you mentioned earlier. What was it about their admission of feeling trapped that made you feel so much more connected to them?

It was because it made them so human. Instead of just being this polished, successful figure, they showed a vulnerability. And in that honesty, I could see their real struggles. And it just made me feel like, okay, this person is more than just a role or an image. And that was really powerful.

It sounds like their willingness to show that struggle was the key to seeing the real person. If that vulnerability is so powerful, why do you think we still work so hard to keep our own struggles hidden in those same kinds of spaces?

Yeah, that's a really good question. I think a big part of it is fear. Fear of being judged or seen as weak, especially in these high stakes environments. We kind of cling to the idea that if we just appear polished enough, we'll be safe. But ironically it's often that crack, that struggle that lets us connect on a deeper level.

You are caught in a tension between the safety of being polished and the connection found in that crack. If you could step into a space where you did not have to worry about being seen as weak, what is the first thing you would stop hiding?

I think the first thing I'd stop hiding is just that occasional uncertainty. You know when I'm not sure, when I'm figuring something out. I think if I let that be visible it would free me up to grow and to actually learn in public rather than feeling like I have to have all the answers right away.
Learning in public instead of having all the answers sounds like a shift from performance to discovery. What is the specific risk you feel you are taking when you admit you do not have the answer?

I think the biggest risk is that I worry someone might see me as less competent or less credible, especially in a professional setting. But I guess the risk is also that if I pretend, I never get the chance to grow or to be truly seen for who I am.

You are weighing the risk of being seen as less competent against the risk of never being truly seen at all. If you chose to prioritize being truly seen, what is the very first thing you would do differently in your next professional interaction?

I think the first thing I would do is at the beginning of a meeting or a conversation, I would name the fact that I'm still figuring something out. I might say, hey, I don't have all the answers yet, but I'm exploring this and just let that be okay. And from there, I think real trust can build.
That simple admission changes the entire dynamic of a room. If you lead with that, how do you think it would change the way others show up around you?

I think it would actually give them permission to be more completely open too. If I show that I can be vulnerable, it creates this space where others feel safer to share what they don't know. And that I think would lead to a much richer and more collaborative environment.

It sounds like your vulnerability would act as a kind of invitation for others to drop their own masks. If that kind of environment is what you truly value, why does it still feel so difficult to be the one who takes that first step?

I think it's because there's still a tiny fear of being misunderstood or of that openness being taken advantage of. But I think the more I remind myself that this is a strength, not a weakness, the easier it becomes. And every time I take that step, it feels a bit less hard.

Taking that step feels a bit less hard each time. You are shifting from seeing vulnerability as something to fear, to seeing it as a strength that builds trust. Is there a moment you can remember where you took that step and it felt surprisingly easy?
